problem_summary: 2018 MCM Problem A (continuous): model how High-Frequency (3-30 MHz) radio "skywave" signals propagate by bouncing between the ionosphere and the Earth's surface, accounting for losses, and determine how many hops a signal can make and how far it travels — comparing reflections off calm vs. turbulent ocean and smooth vs. mountainous land, and finding the maximum effective transmission distance from a coastal station to a ship at sea.
models_used: Ray-tracing / geometric-optics skywave path model on a SPHERICAL Earth and spherical ionospheric shell (80-2000 km), using law-of-sines geometry (Eq. 3-4) to get the transmitter-to-ionosphere leg

Refractive ray-bending integral through the ionosphere: closed-form integrals for the swept angle 2Theta' (Eq. 9) and arc length S (Eq. 5) using Appleton-style refractive index mu = sqrt(1 - 80.5*N/f_v^2) (Eq. 10), driven by NASA International Reference Ionosphere (IRI) electron-density data
Link-budget / signal-strength model in decibels: P_Rx = P_Tx + G_Tx + L_free + L_absorption + L_reflection + G_Rx (Eq. 22), evaluated after every bounce against a 10 dB link-margin cutoff
Non-deviative ionospheric absorption model (D-region): L_absorption = 8.69*S*k with k from electron density N, collision frequency nu, angular frequency omega (Eq. 15)

Surface-reflection loss via Rayleigh roughness criterion: phase spread Delta_psi = 4*pi*Delta_h*sin(theta_i)/lambda (Eq. 16), amplitude reduction Delta_A = exp(-Delta_psi^2) (Eq. 17), Fresnel complex reflectivity coefficient rho for perpendicular polarization (Eq. 18), giving L_reflection = 10*log10(rho*(Delta_A)^2) (Eq. 19)

Vector ray-tracing for the post-bounce direction: local surface normals via MATLAB surfnorm, Law of Reflection r = i + 2cos(theta_local)*n (Eq. 20-21), averaging local reflection vectors (shadowed negative-z vectors discarded) and converting to azimuth/elevation with cart2sph

Terrain generation: real SRTM (Shuttle Radar Topography Mission, 1 arcsec) elevation data for land; procedurally generated ocean wave fields using the Phillips spectrum for a fully-developed sea (adapted MATLAB simulation) for calm/rough/super-turbulent water
Great-circle geodesy to map hop distance to lat/long landing points (Eq. 12-13)

Monte Carlo simulation: thousands of randomized trials (10000 calm-sea first bounces, 1000 for other cases) over random transmitter locations, bearings, and a 0-60 deg elevation sweep in 0.1 deg steps
Two-sample t-tests for hypothesis testing on differences in mean distance, power, and bounce count across conditions
key_techniques: Decomposed the full signal path into modular legs (ground-to-ionosphere straight ray / curved refractive arc / ionosphere-to-ground / surface bounce) and chained them iteratively, re-running the link budget at each interface — a clean state-machine that terminates when power drops below 10 dB

Deliberate complexity budgeting: stated explicitly that they put detail where it matters (ionosphere refraction + absorption, terrain reflection geometry) and simplified where it doesn't (free-space loss, polarization, Doppler, magnetic effects) — and justified each simplification physically (e.g. rain/molecular absorption only matters above 1 GHz)

Grounded the model in REAL external datasets rather than toy functions: NASA IRI electron density (with day/night structure from a fixed epoch) and USGS SRTM topography, plus physically-based Phillips-spectrum wave generation

Used the Rayleigh roughness criterion as the unifying lever to treat ocean turbulence and terrain ruggedness with the SAME math, just varying Delta_h — letting them answer the calm-vs-turbulent and smooth-vs-mountainous sub-questions consistently

Statistical rigor: every comparison backed by a two-sample t-test with reported p-values (e.g. 0.0291 calm-vs-turbulent power; ~0 for distance), and honest reporting when a difference was NOT significant (p=0.39 power smooth-vs-rugged; p=0.64 turbulent-vs-super-turbulent)

Validation against domain knowledge before trusting results: reproduced the known 'greyline'/day-night terminator preference for skywave propagation by overlaying simulated bounce locations on the electron-density map (Fig 8-9), and checked hop distances fell in the literature-expected 2500-5000 km range

Parameter-space sweep that discovered the dominant trade-off: co-varying frequency and elevation angle revealed a narrow 'functional line' in (angle, freq) space — off it, signals either punch through to space or are absorbed — identifying these two as the most sensitive inputs
why_it_won: "It reads like a real engineering study rather than a homework solution. The team correctly identified the physics insight that wins the problem — the ionosphere, not the surface, dominates skywave behavior (15-20 dB loss vs <1 dB at the ground) — and built a faithful, data-grounded, end-to-end simulator around it. Judges reward exactly this combination: (1) a defensible, physically-justified modeling-vs-computation trade-off articulated up front; (2) use of authoritative real data (NASA IRI, USGS SRTM) instead of invented surfaces; (3) statistical hypothesis testing with p-values on every claim, including the intellectual honesty to report null results; (4) genuine validation against an independent known phenomenon (the greyline terminator) before drawing conclusions; and (5) a discovered, non-obvious result (the (angle, frequency) functional line and the dead-zone explanation for ships at sea) that directly and concretely answers the prompt's practical question. The whole thing is also computationally efficient (1000 trials in minutes), demonstrating the model is actually usable."
section_highlights: "Strong, layered structure: a one-page summary that already states quantitative answers (max 2 hops, 0.72 dB calm-vs-turbulent gap, 1622 km reach, greyline conclusion) so a judge knows the payoff immediately. A 'Background' section that teaches the relevant physics (ionospheric D/E/F layers, specular vs diffuse reflection) so the modeling choices feel earned. An explicit, bulleted Assumptions section where each assumption carries a physical justification and a citation, not just a bare statement. A clear 'Existing Models' survey framing analytical-vs-numerical trade-offs, positioning their own choices. Consistent nomenclature table. A well-organized Application (Parts I/II/III) → Results → Sensitivity flow that mirrors the problem's sub-parts one-to-one. A required Letter that translates results into plain operational advice (where dead-zones occur, effective angle/frequency). Heavy, well-captioned figures: annotated ray-bending diagram, terrain meshes with incident/normal/reflected vectors drawn, bounce-location-over-electron-density heatmaps, and box plots for every sensitivity sweep. A balanced, candid Strengths/Weaknesses/Future-Work section."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Terrain is decoupled from geography: every bounce is forced onto a pre-chosen surface type (all-water or all-land), and the lat/long is used ONLY for ionosphere lookup and distance — so a signal's actual landing surface is not respected. This undercuts physical realism of the multi-hop paths

Used a single fixed ionosphere epoch (Jan 1, 2000/2001, 1:30 AM GMT) to get a static day/night snapshot; the model cannot evolve the ionosphere in time within a run, so it can't model how a real contact changes hour-to-hour despite acknowledging that variation matters
Only a tiny 10x10 m terrain/wave patch is simulated per bounce, so very local features dominate and large-scale surface geometry is lost; the team flags this but it limits the reflection statistics

Inconsistent/abrupt 'effective' recommendation: the body's sensitivity sweep concludes the best (angle, freq) is near 10 deg / 20 MHz, yet Parts I-III are run at the arbitrary default 30 deg / 3 MHz, and the Letter awkwardly carries both — a mild internal coherence gap
Applies a perpendicular-polarization Fresnel reflection coefficient (Eq. 18) while explicitly assuming the signal is unpolarized — an admitted physical inconsistency

Several reported figures look internally inconsistent or mislabeled (e.g. summary says '0.72 dB less for turbulent' but Table 1 shows a 25.54 vs 24.82 dB = 0.72 dB gap that is fine, yet Fig 9's 'probability of escaping to space' curve is non-monotonic and hard to reconcile with the narrative), suggesting some rushed figure/data QA
Ignores Doppler shift, geomagnetic field effects, polarization rotation, and multipath outside the ground bounce — reasonable scoping but means absolute loss values are approximate
Cited a StackOverflow/StackExchange wave-simulation snippet as a data source — pragmatic but a weaker provenance than the peer-reviewed/government datasets used elsewhere
reusable_patterns: Lead the summary with concrete numeric answers and the single key insight, before any methods — give judges the payoff on page one
Write an explicit 'complexity budget' paragraph: name where you add detail, where you simplify, and justify each with physics + a citation; this preempts the 'why didn't you model X' critique
Anchor a continuous/physical model in authoritative real datasets (government/NASA/USGS) instead of invented analytic surfaces — and say which fields/resolution you used
Find ONE physical lever that unifies several sub-questions (here the Rayleigh roughness Delta_h handled both ocean turbulence and land ruggedness) so the same machinery answers multiple parts
Pair every quantitative claim with a hypothesis test and a p-value, and explicitly report when a difference is NOT significant — honesty about null results reads as rigor, not weakness
Validate against an independent, literature-known phenomenon before reporting your own results (the greyline/day-night terminator check is a textbook example of credible validation)

Run a deliberate sensitivity sweep over the two most physically-coupled inputs to surface a non-obvious trade-off surface (the (angle, frequency) functional line), then translate it into operational guidance

Build the model as a modular, iterative state machine (leg-by-leg, re-evaluate a single budget/threshold at each interface, terminate on a clear stopping condition) — clean to explain and easy to extend
Report runtime/computational cost as a strength ('1000 trials in minutes on a laptop') to show the model is actually usable, not just correct
Use annotated geometry diagrams (the ray-bending figure, vector-on-mesh reflection plots) so reviewers can verify the geometry visually rather than parsing equations alone
